---
title: "First Project"
description: "Create and run your first Deno project using the built-in project initializer."
---

## Initialize a project

Deno ships with a project scaffolding command that creates a working project structure in seconds.

```bash
deno init my_project
```

This creates a new directory `my_project/` with three files:

```
my_project/
├── deno.json
├── main.ts
└── main_test.ts
```

<Info>
You can also run `deno init` inside an existing empty directory to scaffold in place.
</Info>

## Explore the generated files

### `deno.json`

This is your project's configuration file. It defines tasks, import maps, compiler options, and more.

```json
{
  "tasks": {
    "dev": "deno run --watch main.ts"
  }
}
```

### `main.ts`

The entry point for your application. The generated file exports a simple function:

```typescript
export function add(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a + b;
}

// Learn more at https://docs.deno.com/runtime/manual
if (import.meta.main) {
  console.log("Add 2 + 3 =", add(2, 3));
}
```

### `main_test.ts`

A test file using Deno's built-in test runner:

```typescript
import { assertEquals } from "jsr:@std/assert";
import { add } from "./main.ts";

Deno.test(function addTest() {
  assertEquals(add(2, 3), 5);
});
```

## Run the program

```bash
cd my_project
deno main.ts
```

Output:

```
Add 2 + 3 = 5
```

## Run with watch mode

During development, use the `--watch` flag to restart automatically on file changes:

```bash
deno run --watch main.ts
```

Or use the task defined in `deno.json`:

```bash
deno task dev
```

## Run tests

```bash
deno test
```

Output:

```
running 1 test from ./main_test.ts
addTest ... ok (3ms)

ok | 1 passed | 0 failed (11ms)
```

Deno's test runner is built in — no separate testing framework needed.

## Using JavaScript instead of TypeScript

TypeScript is the recommended default in Deno, but JavaScript works too. Rename the files to `.js` and remove the type annotations:

```javascript
// main.js
export function add(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

if (import.meta.main) {
  console.log("Add 2 + 3 =", add(2, 3));
}
```

```bash
deno main.js
```

## Run a program from a URL

Deno can run code directly from a URL — useful for one-off scripts and examples:

```bash
deno run https://examples.deno.land/hello-world.ts
```

<Warning>
Only run programs from URLs you trust. Deno will still apply permission flags, but always review the source first.
</Warning>
